examples.title: Basic Image Acquisition Process
examples.description: Demonstrates the fundamental steps in converting analog scene to digital image
examples.code:
PROCEDURE ImageAcquisition
INPUT: Optical scene (light rays)
OUTPUT: Digital image matrix f[x][y]

BEGIN
 // Step 1: Light capture
 photons[] ← CaptureLightThroughLens()
 
 // Step 2: Sensor integration
 charges[] ← ConvertPhotonsToCharge(photons)
 
 // Step 3: Sampling (spatial discretization)
 FOR x ← 0 TO Width-1 DO
 FOR y ← 0 TO Height-1 DO
 pixel[x][y] ← SampleAtPosition(x, y)
 END FOR
 END FOR
 
 // Step 4: Quantization (amplitude discretization)
 FOR x ← 0 TO Width-1 DO
 FOR y ← 0 TO Height-1 DO
 f[x][y] ← Quantize(pixel[x][y], BitDepth)
 END FOR
 END FOR
 
 RETURN f[][]
END
examples.explanation: This pseudocode shows the four key stages: light capture by sensor, conversion to charge, spatial sampling to create pixel grid, and quantization to convert continuous values to discrete digital levels.

examples.title: Calculate Image File Size
examples.description: Algorithm to compute expected file size for a digital image
examples.code:
PROCEDURE CalculateFileSize(width, height, bitDepth, compression)
INPUT: Image dimensions, bit depth, compression type
OUTPUT: File size in bytes

BEGIN
 // Calculate uncompressed size
 totalPixels ← width × height
 bitsPerPixel ← bitDepth
 totalBits ← totalPixels × bitsPerPixel
 uncompressedBytes ← totalBits / 8
 
 // Apply compression ratio
 IF compression = "JPEG" THEN
 compressionRatio ← 0.1 // ~10% of original
 ELSE IF compression = "PNG" THEN
 compressionRatio ← 0.5 // ~50% of original (lossless)
 ELSE IF compression = "BMP" THEN
 compressionRatio ← 1.0 // No compression
 ELSE
 compressionRatio ← 1.0
 END IF
 
 fileSize ← uncompressedBytes × compressionRatio
 RETURN fileSize
END

// Example: 1920×1080, 24-bit, JPEG
// 1920 × 1080 × 24 = 49,766,400 bits
// = 6,220,800 bytes uncompressed
// = ~622 KB after JPEG compression
examples.explanation: This algorithm demonstrates how file size is calculated based on resolution (pixels), bit depth (color information), and compression type. Shows why high-resolution images have large file sizes.

examples.title: Pixel Value Access in Image Matrix
examples.description: Demonstrates how pixels are accessed and stored in digital image representation
examples.explanation: Shows how images are mathematically represented as 2D matrices. Each pixel's position is defined by (x,y) coordinates, and color images have multiple matrices (one per channel).
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) for single pixel access, O(n) for traversing all pixels

examples.title: Quantization Process
examples.description: Demonstrates how continuous intensity values are quantized to discrete levels
examples.explanation: This shows how continuous analog signals (like charge in a sensor) are converted to discrete digital values. The bit depth determines how many distinct levels are available.
